title: TENANT
byline: Bulk freighter 'Amaranth'. The crew left their dinner warm.

character.backstory: Nine years riding bulk haulers between the belt stations, the kind of work where you learn a ship by the cradle and the conduit, not the crew manifest. You sign on, you stow the load, you ride to the next dock with people whose names you barely catch. You know how a clamp holds a hull and how to make it let go - which is the only reason you are not screaming yet.

watching: THEY MEANT TO COME RIGHT BACK.
IT SIGNED ON AT THE LAST DOCK.

wake.title: Galley
wake.text:
You surface from a sleep you don't remember choosing, cheek stuck to a galley table. Eleven trays. Steam still rises off the nearest one - a spoon laid down mid-bite, a cup with the ring of it still wet.

No one is at the table. No one answers. You signed on at the last dock - cargo engineer, here for the load, not the company - and all you want is one voice answering back.

choices.text: Sit up, and take in the room.

galley.title: Galley
galley.text: Eleven trays down the table, the nearest still warm to the back of your hand, a spoon set down mid-bite, a cup ringed wet. The chairs are pushed out and empty. A single work glove lies on the deck by the hatch, palm up, fingers half-curled, set down by someone who meant to come straight back.

choices.text: Check the galley's wall-mounted first-aid station.
Go to the hatch and out into the corridor.

galley_glove.title: Galley
galley_glove.text: You crouch and pick it up. It is warm. Not galley-warm - hand-warm, the heat of a grip that let go just before you woke. The fingers hold their half-curl against your palm, still shaped to whatever they were carrying. You put it back, palm up, exactly where it lay. It seems wise not to be the one who moved it.

corridor.title: Spinal corridor
corridor.text:
The spinal corridor runs the length of the Amaranth under tired strip-lights that buzz as you pass beneath them. A duty roster is pinned to a board, eleven names against eleven jobs. Ordinary. The kind of ordinary somebody walked out of in the middle of.

Doors lead off it: crew quarters to port, the cargo hold to starboard, the engine room aft. A bulkhead hatch ahead stands open on the shuttle bay.

sanityText.40: The corridor is longer than it was. You are sure of it the way you are sure of a tooth that has started to ache. The strip-lights buzz, then hush as you pass, as though they would rather not draw attention to you. You read the roster again. Eleven names, and none of them yours, and one of them - you are fairly sure - is new.
sanityText.20: The strip-lights dim and brighten, dim and brighten, slow as breath. You read the roster and the names read you back. They were never crew. They were tenants, like you, and the ship is patient, and there is always room.

quarters.title: Crew quarters
quarters.text: Six bunks, blankets thrown back, the warm hollows of people who got up and never lay down again. A music player on a shelf has run off the end of its track and hisses static at the empty room. A personnel locker hangs open beside the nearest bunk: a photo of a coast taped inside the door, a lanyard hook below it where the crew kept their access to the shuttle.

sanityText.40: Six bunks. The hollows in the blankets are the right shape for sleepers and too deep for sleep, pressed down by a weight that stayed long after a body's warmth would have faded. The music player hisses; under the hiss, if you let yourself, you can hear it keeping time with your breathing. The personnel locker beside the nearest bunk hangs open: a photo of a coast, a lanyard hook below it, the whole arrangement laid out the way bait is laid out.

choices.text: Lift the access chip from the locker.
Switch off the hissing music player.

quarters_chip.title: Crew quarters
quarters_chip.text: You lift the lanyard clear. The chip swings once against the locker door and stops dead - no after-sway, none of the settling a hung thing should do. The quarters hold their breath around you. Nothing moves, and the room is satisfied anyway: an offer taken, terms agreed. You pocket the chip and keep your eyes off the bunks on your way out.

quarters_music.title: Crew quarters
quarters_music.text: Your thumb finds the switch and the static stops. For one second the quarters are truly silent. Then the same track starts again from somewhere deeper in the ship - the same hiss, the same dead air where the song should end - and there is no speaker over there for it to come from. When you take your thumb off the switch, the shelf player resumes mid-breath, as if you had never touched it.

hold.title: Cargo hold
hold.text: The hold is containers stacked six high, the top tiers lost where the lights give out. This part you understand: the load plan, the lashings, the cold vent breathing down the stacks to pool at your ankles. Most are sealed; a few have cracked and spilled packing foam across the deck. A maintenance station is bolted to the bulkhead - tool rack, first-aid box, a charging hook on the wall for the hand-light it docks. Off in the dark, one open container is dripping.

sanityText.40: The containers climb into a dark the lights don't reach, and they are stowed wrong now - leaning inward, the way trees lean over a path that wants you to keep walking. Foam drifts down through the cold. You tell yourself it is foam. The hold keeps not agreeing.

choices.text: Take the torch from the charging hook.
Take the bolt cutters from the tool rack.
Cross to the open container that is dripping.
choices.locked: Cross to the dripping container. (the far stacks are lightless - you need the torch from its charging hook)

hold_container.title: Cargo hold
hold_container.text: The torch beam finds the container and the dripping resolves: the seal itself is sweating, warmer than the vent air, beading where the cold crosses it. You lean over and look in. Gloves. Hundreds of work gloves, paired and folded, each with the half-curl of the one by the galley hatch, every one warm, every one holding the shape of a hand that is not in it. As you watch, the topmost pair flexes, slow, closing on nothing, and settles. You step back. You do not run. Running is a kind of attention.

choices.text: Reach in and lift one glove out.

hold_glove.title: Cargo hold
hold_glove.text: Your fingers close on the topmost glove and lift it free. It is warm to the wrist and it keeps its shape - the curl of fingers, the press of a palm - and as you raise it to the light it tightens around your own hand, gently, the way you would squeeze a hand you were glad to see. You drop it. It falls back among its hundreds and you cannot tell which one was yours.

engine.title: Engine room
engine.text: The drive is a wall of warm steel that ticks as it idles. You find the clamp junction without looking for it - a fat conduit running aft off the bus toward the docking cradle. You know the circuit by heart: lock-power down that run holds the cradle shut, and a shut cradle lets no shuttle leave the Amaranth. A diagnostic screen scrolls green. Everything nominal. Nobody home.

sanityText.40: The drive ticks like something swallowing. A fat conduit runs along the wall toward the docking cradle, and you do not like looking at it - it has the full, fed look of a thing that has carried something thicker than lock-power down toward the bay. The diagnostic screen scrolls green. Everything nominal. The word has stopped meaning anything.

choices.text: Cut the clamp conduit with the bolt cutters.
choices.locked: Cut the clamp conduit to kill the cradle's lock-power. (bare-handed you'll never part it - you need the bolt cutters from the cargo hold)

engine_cut.title: Engine room
engine_cut.text: The cutters bite. A flat bang, and the conduit goes dark, and aft of you the docking clamp lets go with a shudder you feel through the deck plates. Lock-power gone. The cradle will release the shuttle now - if its console agrees to recognise you. The drive's ticking falters, one beat, as though the ship felt that.

engine_log.title: Engine room
engine_log.text: You scroll back past the nominal greens to the last shift. The entries start ordinary - coolant, course, a joke logged by someone bored. Then: "crew complement 11, confirmed 11, recount 12, recount 12, recount 12." The same hand, logging the same recount, once a minute for an hour. Then nothing. You stop reading. You do not want to picture where the twelfth one stood while she counted.

bay.title: Shuttle bay
bay.text: The bay is colder than the rest of the ship. A single short-haul shuttle sits in its cradle, hatch open, ready, as though someone had been about to board and thought better of it. You know this console's checklist by heart: it will not release until you give it a valid access chip and the docking clamp is unpowered.

sanityText.40: The bay is cold enough to ache. The shuttle waits in its cradle with its hatch open like a mouth, and you can no longer tell whether it is offering you a way out or simply hungry the same patient way the rest of the ship is hungry. The console wants its two things. A chip. The clamp dead.

choices.text: Insert the chip and release the shuttle.
choices.locked: Insert the chip and release. (the console needs the crew's access chip, and the docking clamp's lock-power has to be cut first)

choices.text: Force the hatch and seal yourself inside without clearance.
Open the bay's outer doors and step out into the cold.

ending.text:
The chip reads green. The clamp is already dead, so the cradle opens its arms, and the shuttle drops free of the Amaranth. You burn hard for the black before the console can change its mind.

Through the port the freighter shrinks - every light still on, every system nominal, eleven trays still set and steaming. The ship wanted a twelfth name on its roster and you gave it the cut clamp instead. You are awake. You got out.

You wrench the hatch shut, dog it from the inside, and slump into the pilot's seat, breathing hard, safe. The console glows red. You skipped the part that mattered: the clamp still grips the cradle, drawing live lock-power, and a shuttle in a powered clamp does not move. It will never move. You sealed yourself into the one box on the ship the clamp holds tightest.

Behind the hatch the Amaranth settles into a new and comfortable stillness, the way a host settles once a guest has stopped trying to leave. You wait. The bay warms. Somewhere a roster updates to read twelve.

The outer doors part on a wedge of stars and the air leaves the bay in one long sigh and takes you with it. The cold is kinder than the warmth ever was. Out here nothing waits. Out here there is nothing at all, and you open your arms to it.

The bay reseals behind you. The trays stay warm. The ship has room.
